skill.name: setting-up-sending-domain
skill.description: Guides Mailtrap sending domain setup, DNS record publication, verification troubleshooting, provider-specific documentation, and compliance steps before live email sending.
skill.summary: Set up and verify Mailtrap sending domains with correct DNS records and provider guidance.
skill.author: mailtrap

security_audit.summary: The static command execution and weak cryptography findings are false positives caused by Markdown inline code, DNS terminology, and email authentication guidance. The network findings are legitimate because the skill documents authenticated Mailtrap API calls and DNS provider automation, so publication is reasonable with a medium-risk warning.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated Mailtrap and DNS API Operations
medium_findings.description: The skill describes using bearer-token authenticated Mailtrap API calls and DNS provider APIs to list, create, and verify sending domains. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but misuse could change production email and DNS configuration.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly describe authenticated API access and programmatic DNS publication. The behavior matches the stated Mailtrap setup purpose, so it is a controlled operational risk rather than malicious activity.

low_findings.title: False Positive Command Execution Matches
low_findings.description: The static Ruby or shell backtick findings are Markdown inline code markers and references to tools or identifiers. No executable script, Ruby code, command interpolation, or hidden shell execution was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown and the flagged syntax appears inside prose or inline code formatting. I found no executable source file or instruction that runs backtick-expanded shell code.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptography Matches
low_findings.description: The static weak cryptography findings appear to match email authentication and DNS documentation terms, not code that implements cryptographic algorithms. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, insecure cipher use, or custom crypto.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are documentation about domain setup, provider guides, and DNS proxy caveats. There is no executable cryptographic implementation in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: External Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to Mailtrap documentation, Mailtrap API endpoints, and DNS provider documentation. These links are expected for a setup guide, but users should verify domains before sharing tokens or changing DNS records.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible and consistent with the Mailtrap sending-domain workflow. I found no unrelated endpoint or data exfiltration destination.

content.value_statement: Sending domain setup fails when DNS records, provider settings, or verification steps are incomplete. This skill guides Mailtrap users through setup, API checks, DNS publication, and provider-specific troubleshooting.
actual_capabilities: Explains when a Mailtrap sending domain must be added before live sending.
Guides users to copy every required DNS record from the Mailtrap UI or API.
Distinguishes root domains from subdomains used in From addresses.
References Mailtrap provider guides for Cloudflare, Route 53, Google Cloud DNS, and other hosts.
Suggests DNS visibility checks when verification remains pending.
Describes compliance and DNS-only proxy caveats after records are created.
limitations: Does not verify DNS records by itself without access to external DNS tools or APIs.
Does not replace Mailtrap UI values, which are unique for each sending domain.
Does not guarantee DNS propagation timing across registrars and resolvers.
Does not safely rotate or store Mailtrap API tokens for users.

use_cases.title: Launch a New Sending Domain
use_cases.description: Follow a clear checklist to add a domain, copy every DNS record, and complete Mailtrap verification before sending live email.

use_cases.title: Debug Pending Verification
use_cases.description: Review DNS records, hostname choices, propagation status, and proxy settings when Mailtrap verification does not complete.

use_cases.title: Automate Domain Provisioning
use_cases.description: Use the documented Mailtrap API flow and DNS provider APIs to create repeatable domain setup workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Domain Setup
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup through the Mailtrap UI
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me set up a Mailtrap sending domain for example.com. Give me a checklist for the UI and DNS provider steps.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Root or Subdomain
prompt_templates.scenario: Confirm the correct hostname before adding records
prompt_templates.prompt: I will send from notifications.example.com. Should I add the root domain or the subdomain in Mailtrap, and why?

prompt_templates.title: Troubleshoot DNS Verification
prompt_templates.prompt: Mailtrap verification is pending after I added the records. Walk me through checks for DNS propagation, copied values, and proxy settings.

prompt_templates.title: Plan API Automation
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced provisioning with Mailtrap and DNS provider APIs
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a safe workflow to create Mailtrap sending domains through the API and publish returned DNS records through my DNS provider.

output_examples.input: I need to add notifications.example.com as a Mailtrap sending domain.
output: Confirm that notifications.example.com is the exact From hostname.
Add that hostname in Mailtrap Sending Domains.
Copy every DNS record shown by Mailtrap into the active DNS zone.
Wait for public DNS visibility, then verify in Mailtrap.
Complete the compliance step if Mailtrap requests it.

output_examples.input: Mailtrap still says my domain is not verified.
output: Compare each record type, name, and value against the Mailtrap UI.
Check public DNS visibility with a DNS lookup tool.
Disable proxying for verification records unless Mailtrap documentation allows it.
Confirm that records were added to the zone serving the From domain.

output_examples.input: I want to automate setup for customer domains.
output: Use a Mailtrap token with the minimum required scope.
Create or list sending domains through the Mailtrap account API.
Read the returned DNS records and publish them through the DNS provider API.
Poll verification status and surface pending records for manual review.
Avoid logging bearer tokens or full secret values.

best_practices: Copy every DNS record exactly as Mailtrap provides it, including type, name, and value.
Use DNS-only mode for verification records when a provider offers proxying.
Limit API token scope and avoid exposing bearer tokens in logs or prompts.
anti_patterns: Adding only selected SPF or DKIM records instead of the complete Mailtrap record set.
Adding the root domain when the actual From address uses a subdomain.
Automating DNS changes without a review step for production domains.

faq.question: Do I need a verified domain before sending live email?
faq.answer: Yes. Mailtrap requires the sending domain to be added and verified before sending from that domain.

faq.question: Should I add the root domain or a subdomain?
faq.answer: Add the exact hostname used in the From address. Use the subdomain if mail comes from that subdomain.

faq.question: Can this skill fetch my Mailtrap DNS records automatically?
faq.answer: It can explain the API flow, but actual fetching requires a valid Mailtrap token and account ID.

faq.question: Why does verification stay pending after I added records?
faq.answer: Common causes include propagation delay, copied value errors, wrong DNS zone, or proxied verification records.

faq.question: Which DNS providers are covered?
faq.answer: The skill references Mailtrap guides for Cloudflare, AWS Route 53, Google Cloud DNS, Squarespace, GoDaddy, Namecheap, and DigitalOcean.

faq.question: Is API automation safe for production domains?
faq.answer: It can be safe with least-privilege tokens, secret handling, audit logs, and human review before DNS changes.
